title: Birthday Line

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A manager gives a free ticket to the FIRST person in line whose birthday matches someone who already bought a ticket ahead of them. You can choose your position in line.
rules: All birthdays are uniformly random over 365 days, independent across people.
If you are in position n, you win if and only if: the first n-1 people all have DISTINCT birthdays, AND your birthday matches one of theirs.
Earlier positions might 'snipe' the prize before you get a chance.
setup.question: What position n should you choose to maximize your probability of winning?

learningObjectives: Compute P(n) = P(first n-1 distinct) * P(your birthday matches one of theirs).
Find the optimal n by checking when P(n) > P(n-1) AND P(n) > P(n+1).
Solve the two resulting quadratic inequalities.

stages.label: Set up P(n)
stages.prompt: If you're at position n, you win iff (the first n-1 are distinct) AND (your birthday matches one of those n-1). What is P(n)?
stages.explanation: First factor: P(first n-1 all distinct birthdays). Second factor: P(your birthday matches one of n-1 specific dates) = (n-1)/365.

stages.label: Find P(n) > P(n-1) condition
stages.prompt: When does increasing your position from n-1 to n improve P? Setting up P(n)/P(n-1) > 1 and simplifying gives a quadratic in n. What is it?

options.text: (367 - n)(n - 1) > 365(n - 2),  which simplifies to n^2 - 3n - 363 < 0,  so n <= 20.

stages.explanation: P(n)/P(n-1) compares (365-n+2)/365 * (n-1)/(n-2) > 1. Cross-multiply and simplify: (367-n)(n-1) > 365(n-2). Expand and rearrange to get n^2 - 3n - 363 < 0 (note the sign flip). Roots ≈ 20.6, so n <= 20.

stages.label: Find P(n) > P(n+1) condition
stages.prompt: When does P(n) > P(n+1) (so increasing further hurts)?
stages.explanation: P(n+1)/P(n) similarly: (366-n)/365 * n/(n-1) < 1. Simplify to n^2 - n - 365 > 0 (roots ≈ 19.6 and -18.6), giving n >= 20.

stages.label: Combine the two conditions
stages.prompt: Both conditions (n <= 20 AND n >= 20) hold simultaneously only at one value of n. What is it?
stages.explanation: Only n = 20 satisfies both inequalities simultaneously. P(n) is unimodal in n; the peak is at n = 20.

stages.label: Commit
stages.explanation: 20th in line. The trade-off: too early, you have nothing to match against; too late, the first match has already been triggered by someone before you.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Compute P(20) numerically. How big is the winning probability?
What if there are 366 days in a year? Does the optimal position shift?
What's the optimal position for general 'days in year' D? (Hint: it's around sqrt(D).)
